topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

The realtor, a whirlwind of forced enthusiasm, had pointed out the 'unique architectural features' of the old Victorian. I nodded along, my gaze drifting over the peeling wallpaper and the faint, almost invisible scuff marks near the baseboards. It was just an old house, full of character, nothing more. I signed the papers, the pen scratching a dull rhythm against the thick parchment. Later that evening, while unpacking the last of my boxes, I noticed a faint, almost imperceptible discoloration on the underside of a loose floorboard in the study. I knelt down, running a fingertip over the spot. It felt like dried mud, nothing more. I straightened up, brushed the dust off my hands, and decided it was probably just old water damage. The whole experience had been tedious, a necessary hurdle before settling into the next phase.

[story 2]

I walked through the rooms with a practiced detachment, cataloging the damage as I went. The smell of stale air and old wood hung heavy, a familiar scent in this tired neighborhood. The previous owners had left a mess, which was expected. I didn't linger on the dark stain near the fireplace mantel; it was probably just soot from decades of burning wood. My shoulders slumped slightly as I surveyed the sparsely furnished living room. There was a certain weight to the silence in the place, a quiet that suggested something heavy had happened here long ago, but I simply cataloged it as an atmospheric quirk of an old dwelling. I poured myself a drink, the ice clinking softly, and let the unsettling quiet wash over me without offering any real resistance.

[story 3]

The inspector had been thorough, ticking off every structural flaw with clinical precision. I watched him leave, feeling a mild sense of completion. The house was mine now, a blank slate waiting for my renovations. As I moved into the kitchen, I noticed a peculiar pattern in the grout of the back patio—a series of faint, overlapping lines that didn't match any known pattern. I paused, tilting my head. It was odd, certainly, but I dismissed it quickly. Maybe it was just an uneven application of sealant. I walked toward the back door, my steps even, my expression unreadable. There was a low hum of unease, a background static that I chose to tune out, focusing instead on the blueprints for the new kitchen layout.
